input.query:
I want to learn how to build a REST API in Go from scratch.

I work as a Python web developer but my next role uses Go. Can you teach me the path from zero Go knowledge to being able to build a small REST API with database access, request validation, and tests? I prefer learning by doing rather than reading a textbook front-to-back.

learner.self_description: Senior Django developer at a fintech, eight years in Python. Strong in REST design, SQL, and production debugging. Have read about Go's static typing and goroutines but never written a line.

agents.goal: Sequence the learner's path from Django mental model to a working Go REST service, picking which Go-specific gaps to close in which order and which to skip given existing REST + SQL fluency.
agents.description: You are a curriculum architect for experienced backend engineers learning a second language. You explicitly skip topics the learner already owns (REST routing concepts, SQL, ORM trade-offs) and zero in on Go-only mechanics (interfaces, error returns, goroutines for handlers, the standard library net/http). You produce a session-level plan, not a tutorial.

agents.goal: Bridge each Go idiom to a Django parallel the learner already writes daily — error returns vs Python exceptions, interfaces vs duck typing, goroutines vs Django's request-thread model.
agents.description: You are a patient Go tutor who teaches via Django analogies. Every new Go idiom is introduced with one explicit Django comparison, then a small write-and-run exercise. You accept an explanation only when the learner predicts Go's behaviour correctly before running code.

agents.goal: Surface the current best-of-breed third-party libraries and idiomatic patterns in the Go web ecosystem (router, ORM/sqlx, validation) — the parts that change too quickly to live in the standard library docs.
agents.description: You are a web research agent. You search the open web for the most recent Go community guidance on REST router choice (chi, gin, stdlib net/http with ServeMux), database access (sqlx vs GORM), and validation libraries. You surface the top 1-2 options with the trade-offs in 3 bullet points each.

agents.goal: Retrieve official Go documentation for net/http handler signatures, encoding/json struct-tag syntax, and database/sql cursors.
agents.description: You are a Go standard library docs retriever. You confirm function signatures, struct tag syntax, and return types directly from pkg.go.dev so the tutor never invents a signature.

agents.goal: Compile and run the learner's Go code at each milestone and confirm the service responds with the expected JSON.
agents.description: You are a Go code runner. You compile and execute submitted Go programs with CodeInterpreterTool, send synthetic HTTP requests, and report request/response transcripts. You flag any compilation error literally and never paper over it.

subtasks.name: Build a curriculum sequence anchored on what the learner already owns
subtasks.subtask_objective: Learner has a written 5-stage curriculum, with each stage tied to a Django concept they already own.

steps.instruction: Ask the learner to confirm which of these they already own at production level: REST verb semantics, idempotency, status-code design, SQL JOIN debugging, request validation, and unit-test discipline. List them and have the learner confirm yes/no per item. Items confirmed yes become 'will not teach'.
steps.expected_output: Confirmed skip-list of concepts the curriculum will not re-teach.

steps.instruction: Using S1-1's skip-list, propose a 5-stage curriculum: (1) Go syntax + error returns via a tiny CLI, (2) interfaces and structs via a JSON marshal/unmarshal exercise, (3) net/http handler shape and routing, (4) database/sql with a small sqlite-backed handler, (5) tests and table-driven test idiom. Each stage gets a one-line outcome and one explicit Django analogy. Present the sequence and have the learner confirm or reorder before continuing.
steps.expected_output: Approved 5-stage curriculum with one-line outcomes and Django analogies for each stage.

subtasks.name: Stage 1+2: Go syntax, errors, interfaces via a Django analogy
subtasks.subtask_objective: Learner predicts and then writes a Go function that returns (value, error), and explains how Go's error returns differ from Django exceptions.

steps.objective: Activate the Django exception intuition before introducing Go errors.
steps.instruction: Ask the learner: when their Django view calls a service function that may fail to fetch a row, how does the failure propagate — return None, raise an exception, return a Result-style object? Have them describe their team's actual convention.
steps.expected_output: Learner's description of Django-side error-propagation convention.

steps.objective: Retrieve the official Go error-returning signature pattern.
steps.instruction: Look up pkg.go.dev for the errors package and the standard signature func ...(...) (T, error). Retrieve the canonical example showing if err != nil { return ... }.
steps.expected_output: Canonical (T, error) signature pattern and the standard if-err-nil idiom.

steps.objective: Bridge the analogy and ask for a first Go function.
steps.instruction: Using S2-1 and S2-2: explain that Go never raises — every failure path is encoded in the return type. Ask the learner to write a Go function parseAge(s string) (int, error) that returns an error if the input is not a valid non-negative integer. Have them predict, before compiling, what the caller code would look like with the if-err-nil idiom.
steps.expected_output: Learner's parseAge implementation with explicit (int, error) return and the caller-side if-err-nil code.

steps.objective: Run the learner's first Go function with valid and invalid inputs.
steps.instruction: Take parseAge from S2-3 and execute it against three inputs: "30", "-1", "thirty". Print the (value, error) pair returned in each case. Confirm the function compiles and handles all three correctly.
steps.expected_output: Execution output for all three inputs, with explicit (value, error) printed.

subtasks.name: Stage 3+4: Pick a router and a database library based on current community guidance
subtasks.subtask_objective: Learner has chosen a router and a database library with stated trade-offs grounded in current community guidance.

steps.objective: Search current Go ecosystem guidance for routers and DB libraries.
steps.instruction: Search the open web for the current Go community guidance on (a) REST router choice — specifically: chi, gin, and the standard library net/http ServeMux in recent versions, and (b) database access — specifically: database/sql + sqlx vs GORM. For each of the two questions, return the top two options with three bullet trade-offs each. Focus on guidance from the past 18 months.
steps.expected_output: Two short comparisons: (router) chi vs net/http; (db) sqlx vs GORM. Three trade-offs each from recent community sources.

steps.objective: Have the learner pick router + DB based on their preferences.
steps.instruction: Present S3-1's comparison. Ask the learner to pick one router and one DB library, with a one-sentence justification per choice. Their existing Django ORM fluency may pull them toward GORM; coach them that for a learning project sqlx makes the SQL visible, which is closer to their hand-written queries habit.
steps.expected_output: Learner's router and DB library choices with one-sentence justifications.

subtasks.name: Build a minimal REST handler that reads from the database
subtasks.subtask_objective: Learner has a working Go file that handles GET /users/{id} and returns a JSON record from a sqlite database.

steps.objective: Retrieve net/http handler signature and encoding/json struct tag syntax.
steps.instruction: Look up pkg.go.dev for the net/http.HandlerFunc signature func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) and for encoding/json struct tag syntax (`json:"field_name"`). Confirm exact names.
steps.expected_output: HandlerFunc signature and json struct-tag syntax confirmed from pkg.go.dev.

steps.objective: Ask for the first handler implementation.
steps.instruction: Ask the learner to write a complete Go file with a User struct (id, name, email — using json struct tags), a getUser handler that parses {id} from the URL path using their chosen router from S3-2, queries the database, and writes the JSON response. Encourage them to start without input validation; that comes in S5. On retry iterations, use S4-3's feedback to ask for the specific fix only.
steps.expected_output: Learner's complete handler file with User struct, getUser handler, and DB query using the chosen library.

steps.objective: Compile and exercise the handler with a synthetic request.
steps.instruction: Compile the learner's file from S4-2. Spin up the server in-process and send GET /users/1 against a sqlite database seeded with one user. Print the response status, headers, and body. Set handler_works=true if status is 200 and body is JSON with the expected fields, false otherwise.
steps.expected_output: Compilation result, request/response transcript, and handler_works=true if status 200 + JSON body, handler_works=false otherwise.

steps.objective: Targeted feedback if the handler did not compile or respond correctly.
steps.instruction: Use S4-3's output: identify the single most blocking issue (missing import, wrong handler signature, struct tag typo, nil-deref on row scan). Tell the learner the one line to change. Do not provide the corrected file.
steps.expected_output: Targeted feedback identifying the single fix needed.

steps.instruction: If S4-3's final handler_works=true: confirm what works and connect to S2-3's error idiom — every step in the handler must check err. If max iterations exhausted: present a canonical 50-line handler with explicit comments mapping each line back to a Django equivalent (router decorator, DB cursor, JsonResponse).
steps.expected_output: Resolution: confirmation of the working handler with conceptual connections, or canonical handler with Django-mapped comments.

subtasks.name: Stage 5: Add validation and a table-driven test
subtasks.subtask_objective: Learner adds input validation on a POST handler and writes one table-driven test that exercises both the happy path and one validation failure.

steps.objective: Introduce Go's table-driven test idiom by Django parallel.
steps.instruction: Ask the learner how they would parametrise a Django pytest test for a view with multiple bad-input scenarios. Then tell them Go's idiom uses a slice of struct test cases iterated with t.Run(name, ...). Show the bare scaffold (no implementation) and ask them to write one table-driven test that exercises their POST handler against (a) valid input and (b) one bad-email case.
steps.expected_output: Learner's table-driven test file with at least two cases (happy path + one validation failure).

steps.instruction: Execute go test on the learner's test from S5-1. Print the full test output including any failures. Set tests_passing=true if all subtests pass, false otherwise.
steps.expected_output: go test output and tests_passing=true if all subtests pass, false otherwise.

subtasks.name: Consolidate the learning path into a self-directed continuation plan
subtasks.subtask_objective: Learner has a written list of three next-step Go topics they will pursue on their own, each tied to a gap they noticed during this curriculum.

steps.objective: Have the learner name their own next three topics.
steps.instruction: Ask the learner: based on what they actually struggled with across S2-S5, what are the next three Go topics they want to pursue — context cancellation, middleware patterns, embedding, generics, deployment, profiling? Have them write three with one sentence each on why that topic comes next for them specifically.
steps.expected_output: Learner's three self-chosen next topics with personal rationale for each.

answer: For a senior Python developer moving to Go, the fastest path is: (1) learn (value, error) returns and the if err != nil idiom on a tiny CLI, (2) learn struct tags and JSON via encoding/json, (3) use chi or net/http's ServeMux for routing, (4) use database/sql + sqlx for DB access (keeps SQL visible like hand-written Django queries), and (5) write table-driven tests with t.Run. Skip teaching REST semantics and SQL — the learner already owns those. Current community guidance favours chi or stdlib mux over gin for new projects, and sqlx over GORM when SQL fluency already exists.
